analogy: Think of "Composition and Structure of Atmosphere" through the chapter's own opening images: SOLAR RADIATION, HEAT BALANCE AND TEMPERATURE D o you feel air around you? Do you know that we live at the bottom of a huge pile of air? We inhale and exhale but we feel the air when it is in motion. You have already... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Composition and Structure of Atmosphere is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that sOLAR RADIATION, HEAT BALANCE AND TEMPERATURE D o you feel air around you?

The text explains that do you know that we live at the bottom of a huge pile of air?

The text explains that we inhale and exhale but we feel the air when it is in motion.

The text explains that you have already learnt about the fact that earth is surrounded by air all around.

The text explains that this envelop of air is atmosphere which is composed of numerous gases.

The text explains that these gases support life over the earth’s surface.

The text explains that the earth receives almost all of its energy from the sun.

The text explains that the earth in turn radiates back to space the energy received from the sun.

The text explains that as a result, the earth neither warms up nor does it get cooled over a period of time.

The text explains that thus, the amount of heat received by different parts of the earth is not the same.

The text explains that this variation causes pressure differences in the atmosphere.

The text explains that this leads to transfer of heat from one region to the other by winds.

The text explains that this chapter explains the process of heating and cooling of the atmosphere and the resultant temperature distribution over the earth’s surface.

The text explains that sOLAR RADIATION The earth’s surface receives most of its energy in short wavelengths.

The text explains that the energy received by the earth is known as incoming solar radiation which in short is termed as insolation.

The text explains that as the earth is a geoid resembling a sphere, the sun’s rays fall obliquely at the top of the atmosphere and the earth intercepts a very small portion of the sun’s energy.

The text explains that the Passage of Solar Radiation through the Atmosphere The atmosphere is largely transparent to short wave solar radiation.

The text explains that the earth after being heated by insolation transmits the heat to the atmospheric layers near to the earth in long wave form.

The text explains that conduction takes place when two bodies of unequal temperature are in contact with one another, there is a flow of energy from the warmer to cooler body.

The text explains that the transfer of heat continues until both the bodies attain the same temperature or the contact is broken.

The text explains that terrestrial Radiation The insolation received by the earth is in short waves forms and heats up its surface.

The text explains that sOLAR RADIATION, HEAT BALANCE AND TEMPERATURE 69 the earth surface.

The text explains that of these, 17 units are radiated to space directly and the remaining 34 units are absorbed by the atmosphere (6 units absorbed directly by the atmosphere, 9 units through convection and turbulence and 19 units through latent heat of condensation).

The text explains that this explains, why the earth neither warms up nor cools down despite the huge transfer of heat that takes place. earth after being heated itself becomes a radiating body and it radiates energy to the atmosphere in long wave form.

The text explains that the long wave radiation is absorbed by the atmospheric gases particularly by carbon dioxide and the other green house gases.

The text explains that thus, the atmosphere is indirectly heated by the earth’s radiation.

The text explains that finally the amount of heat received from the sun is returned to space, thereby maintaining constant temperature at the earth’s surface and in the atmosphere.

The text explains that the latitude : The temperature of a place depends on the insolation received.

The text explains that it has been explained earlier that the insolation varies according to the latitude hence the temperature also varies accordingly.

The text explains that the altitude : The atmosphere is indirectly heated by terrestrial radiation from below.

The text explains that therefore, the places near the sea-level record higher temperature than the places situated at higher elevations.

The text explains that in other words, the temperature generally decreases with increasing height.

The text explains that the rate of decrease of temperature with height is termed as the normal lapse rate.

The text explains that distance from the sea : Another factor that influences the temperature is the location of a place with respect to the sea.

The text explains that therefore, the variation in temperature over the sea is less compared to land.

The text explains that the places situated near the sea come under the moderating influence of the sea and land breezes which moderate the temperature.

The text explains that air-mass and Ocean currents : Like the land and sea breezes, the passage of air masses also affects the temperature.

The text explains that the places, which come under the influence of warm air- masses experience higher temperature and the places that come under the influence of cold air-masses experience low temperature.

The text explains that similarly, the places located on the coast where the warm ocean currents flow record higher temperature than the places located on the coast where the cold currents flow.

The text explains that distribution of Temperature The global distribution of temperature can well be understood by studying the temperature distribution in January and July.

The text explains that the temperature distribution is generally Temperature The interaction of insolation with the atmosphere and the earth’s surface creates heat which is measured in terms of temperature.

The text explains that while heat represents the molecular movement of particles comprising a substance, the temperature is the measurement in degrees of how hot (or cold) a thing (or a place) is.

The text explains that factors Controlling Temperature Distribution The temperature of air at any place is influenced by (i) the latitude of the place; (ii) the altitude of the place; (iii) distance from

The text explains that sOLAR RADIATION, HEAT BALANCE AND TEMPERATURE 71 shown on the map with the help of isotherms.

The text explains that the Isotherms are lines joining places having equal temperature.

The text explains that figure 8.4 (a) and (b) show the distribution of surface air temperature in the month of January and July.

The text explains that in general the effect of the latitude on temperature is well pronounced on the map, as the isotherms are generally parallel to the latitude.

The text explains that over the land the temperature decreases sharply and the isotherms bend towards south in Europe.

The text explains that (iii) Compare the global distribution of temperature in January over the northern and the southern hemisphere of the earth.

The text explains that tabulate the temperature data as given in the climatological table of observatories : (i) Note the altitude, latitude of the observatory and the period for which the mean is calculated.

The text explains that (ii) Define the terms related to temperature as given in the table.

The text explains that (iii) Calculate the daily mean monthly temperature.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Fundamentals of Physical Geography, Chapter 8: Composition and Structure of Atmosphere

prelims_traps: Do not treat SOLAR RADIATION, HEAT BALANCE and AND TEMPERATURE as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Composition and Structure of Atmosphere; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Composition and Structure of Atmosphere through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use SOLAR RADIATION, HEAT BALANCE and AND TEMPERATURE to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Composition and Structure of Atmosphere, which statement is supported by the source text about SOLAR RADIATION, HEAT BALANCE AND TEMPERATURE 69?
options: The solar output received at the top of the atmosphere varies slightly in a year due to the variations in the distance between the...
During its revolution around the sun, the earth is farthest from the sun (152 million km) on 4th July.
On 3rd January, the earth is the nearest to the sun (147 million km).
Therefore, the annual insolation received by the earth on 3rd January is slightly more than the amount received on 4th July.
